content.method: Cost modeling using Hungarian administrative school-finance data combined with Polish reform/reversal cost benchmarks adjusted for scale; scenario analysis comparing full abolition, partial phase-down, and status-quo-plus-investment pathways under current and reformed funding formulas.
content.question: What is the net fiscal and distributional impact of eliminating versus preserving the 6/8-year gimnázium tracks, including direct restructuring costs (school closures, teacher displacement, cohort transition overlap) and opportunity costs of foregone compensatory investment in comprehensive schools?
content.why_it_matters: Decision-makers need a comparative cost-benefit basis across structural (T2, T5), compensatory (T4), and fiscal-incentive (T6) proposals; without this, resource allocation decisions rest on qualitative judgment alone despite very different cost profiles implied by the Polish reversal (7,000 school closures, 10,000 displaced teachers) versus a quality-investment-only path.
